title: Structured Sharing With Layer Deltas
research_question: Can cross-layer redundancy be exploited by sharing sub-block structure and restoring layer individuality with small learned deltas, instead of using blunt low-rank factorization or whole-block sharing?
implementation_notes: Implemented via SHARE_DELTA_* env knobs that alias selected attention, mixer, or MLP submodules to a shared base and attach small low-rank per-layer output deltas.
The shared-submodule aliases are deduped in state_dict so artifact-size accounting stays honest.
This is meant to test structured sharing with preserved layer individuality, not blunt full-block sharing.

candidates.name: ae1-upper-attention-shared-base-plus-delta
candidates.goal: Test whether upper attention blocks reuse enough structure to share a base while keeping small per-layer corrections.
candidates.hypothesis: Upper attention layers may contain shareable structure if each layer still gets a small learned delta.
candidates.what_it_teaches: Whether attention redundancy exists at the sub-block level rather than the whole-block level.
candidates.why_worthy: This is the direct successor to the failed whole-block-sharing idea and attacks the same gap with a more surgical mechanism.
candidates.description: share upper attention bases in layer pairs and restore uniqueness with small deltas

candidates.name: ae2-lower-mixer-shared-base-plus-delta
candidates.goal: Test whether the lower structured-processing stage contains more shareable redundancy than the upper reasoning stage.
candidates.hypothesis: Lower mixer blocks may be more repetitive and therefore better candidates for structured sharing.
candidates.what_it_teaches: Whether the lower stack is the better target for compression-native sharing.
candidates.why_worthy: This directly checks whether the shared-structure opportunity lives in the stage that already looks more overbuilt.
candidates.description: share lower mixer bases in pairs and restore layer individuality with small deltas

candidates.name: ae3-upper-ffn-shared-base-plus-delta
candidates.goal: Test whether FFNs are the real redundancy source once layer-specific deltas are preserved.
candidates.hypothesis: Upper FFNs may share a large common basis while still needing only small layer-specific corrections.
candidates.what_it_teaches: Whether FFN sharing is more viable than attention sharing in this structured form.
candidates.why_worthy: The FFN budget is large enough that even a partial win here would materially change the architecture story.
candidates.description: share upper FFN bases in pairs and restore uniqueness with low-rank deltas

candidates.name: ae4-broader-upper-attention-sharing
candidates.goal: Test whether a broader shared attention basis can work when deltas are still available.
candidates.hypothesis: Attention redundancy may span more than layer pairs if small deltas preserve layer identity.
candidates.what_it_teaches: Whether the useful sharing unit is very local or can stretch across a wider upper band.
candidates.why_worthy: This is the bolder pressure test of the structured-sharing idea, not just the safest pairwise version.
candidates.description: share a broader upper attention basis across the early upper band and keep small per-layer deltas

candidates.name: ae5-share-then-reinvest
candidates.goal: Test whether structured sharing only becomes useful when the saved bytes are reinvested into a stronger backbone component.
candidates.hypothesis: Sharing may be a reallocation mechanism rather than a direct quality win by itself.
candidates.what_it_teaches: Whether structured sharing should be judged as compression alone or as budget redeployment.
candidates.why_worthy: This is the most strategic version of the branch because it asks what the saved capacity should actually buy us.
candidates.description: share lower mixer bases with deltas and reinvest the savings into a wider lower mixer
